title: Open Science for Music Research
text:
1 Open Science for Music Research
This repository offers a guide to Open Science practices for music research, particularly aimed for music psychology and computational musicology, but also relevant for music information retrieval, music education, music technology, and musicology.
crumbs: 1  Open Science for Music Research

title: Open Science for Music Research
text:
1.1 Reasons for creating this resource
In music research, there is room for improvement in transparency and reproducibility, to widen the accessibility and equity with Open access to publications and data ensures that researchers, practitioners, and the public can benefit from scientific knowledge. Also, innovation and collaboration benefits from Open Science. A good teaching material dedicated to music research is missing at the moment and this repo attempts to fill this gap.
crumbs: 1  Open Science for Music Research

text:

Chapter 3 explains the rationale and the broader picture
Chapter 4 covers the availability of research output and publications
Chapter 5 handles the availability of research data
Chapter 6 is about reproducible research, research code and software
Chapter 7 explains the purpose of replicating studies
Chapter 8 covers open peer review and post-publication peer review
Chapter 9 details study preregistration
Chapter 10 offers links to online resources
crumbs: 1  Open Science for Music Research

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.1 Plan and schedule
In music research, particularly for music psychology and computational musicology, we should have a repository of information about Open Science and its all areas (open access publication, open data, open code, registration, and replication). Some of this information has been put together for journal articles (Jensenius, 2021; Moss & Neuwirth, 2021), but having a centralised starting point for this would be helpful for training and awareness.
The repository of information can be best done with collaborative writing and compiling the resources together. The contributors represent various sub-disciplines of music research (music psychology, computational musicology, MIR) and different countries (legal implications and academic practices).

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.1 7.1 The importance of replication
Empirical findings accumulate scientific value not only when they are first reported, but when they can be observed again under comparable conditions. A single study, however carefully designed, provides limited evidence on its own: its results may reflect a genuine effect, but they may equally reflect sampling variability, methodological idiosyncrasies, or the inadvertent influence of researcher decisions that go unreported. The credibility of scientific claims is substantially strengthened when independent researchers, working with fresh samples and potentially varying some procedural details, arrive at similar conclusions.
Concern about the replicability of published findings gained widespread attention following a large coordinated effort to reproduce results from experimental psychology. The Open Science Collaboration (open2015estimating?) attempted to replicate 100 studies published in three leading psychology journals. The findings were sobering: fewer than half of the original effects replicated in the conventional sense of reaching statistical significance with the same direction of effect, and average effect sizes in replication studies were roughly half those of the original reports. Although the precise interpretation of these results has been debated, the project drew attention to the possibility that a substantial proportion of published findings may be less robust than they initially appeared.
Music psychology, as a discipline closely tied to experimental psychology, is not immune to these concerns. Studies in the field frequently involve small or convenience samples, rely on stimuli that are rarely shared or clearly specified, and draw on paradigms developed without cross-cultural validation. At the same time, music research has distinctive features that make replication both particularly valuable and particularly challenging. The relationship between acoustic features and musical experience depends on cultural background, familiarity, and individual differences in ways that may not generalise across populations. These considerations make transparent reporting of methods and materials—and a willingness to replicate key findings—an important part of responsible music research practice.

title: 9  Preregistration
text:
9.1 Existence of systematic bias
In scholarly work, it is not unusual for researchers to prefer certain outcomes over others, to hope that findings support a particular theory, or to give less attention to results that fail to confirm initial expectations. Such tendencies are well documented in the history of science and can be regarded as sources of systematic bias in the research process (Lakens et al., 2024). Preregistration is one approach to reducing such bias. It increases transparency by documenting claims, hypotheses, and analytical plans before the results are known, allowing others to evaluate how conclusions were reached. It may also help reduce publication bias, the tendency for positive or statistically significant findings to be published more frequently than null or negative results.
In music research, as in many other disciplines, there are numerous opportunities for systematic bias to arise. Statistical analyses may be conducted selectively, with researchers choosing among multiple analytical options after inspecting the data. Variables, outcome measures, or subsets of data may be reported opportunistically because they produce more favourable results. Research questions and hypotheses may also shift after the results are known, a practice commonly referred to as HARKing (Hypothesising After the Results are Known). Importantly, such practices are not always intentional. Researchers may be unaware that repeated reanalysis of data, selective reporting, or exploration of numerous analytical pathways can introduce bias into the final conclusions. Nevertheless, examples of these practices are easy to find, and together they illustrate how systematic bias can emerge in empirical research even when researchers act in good faith.

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.3 Writing guidelines

Check CONTRIBUTING.md guide first.
Using markdown text, including markdown references, e.g. [@moss2021fair] linked to references.bib file, and utilising within document links @sec-background. All implemented via Quarto documents at GitHub to compile site/book as needed.
Tracking the progress and issues via GitHub Issues.
Authorship order to be determined based on contribution and made transparent with Contributor Taxonomy.

title: 9  Preregistration
text:
9.2 Definition of preregistration
Preregistration is designed to counteract systematic biases by making key research decisions transparent before data collection or analysis. Following the definition proposed by Lakens et al. (2024), preregistration is:

“a complete description of all information related to planned analyses (including the experimental design, measures, data preprocessing, and when statistical tests will corroborate or falsify predictions) that is demonstrably created without access to the data that will be analysed.”

In practical terms, this means that researchers document their study procedures, hypotheses, and statistical analysis plans before collecting or analysing data. When data already exist, typically because they were collected by other researchers, it is still possible to preregister an analysis plan, provided that researchers can demonstrate they did not have access to the data before developing the plan (Mertens & Krypotos, 2019).
In some areas of music research, such as corpus studies, this principle may be more difficult to uphold because many datasets are openly available and can be accessed without any formal request process. This makes it harder to verify that analytical decisions were specified before researchers examined the data. One way to mitigate this challenge is to maintain open laboratory notebooks or other time-stamped records that document decisions, analyses, and claims as they develop throughout the research process (Scroggie et al., 2023).

title: 9  Preregistration
text:
9.3 Variants of preregistration
Preregistration practices originated in clinical and medical research. Clinical trial registries began to emerge in the late 1990s and became increasingly common during the 2000s as journals and regulators began to require them. These registries provide a searchable record of a study’s aims, design, methods, and planned outcomes before results are known.
Outside clinical research, preregistration has evolved into several formats that differ in complexity and level of scrutiny. These range from brief registrations that document core hypotheses and analyses, to comprehensive preregistrations that specify study procedures and analytical decisions in detail, and ultimately to Registered Reports, where study plans undergo peer review before data collection begins. Although these approaches vary in their requirements, they share the common goal of increasing transparency and reducing opportunities for bias in the research process. Table 9.1 lists five common services for study preregistrations in order of complexity.



Table 9.1: Five preregistration services.





Service
Typical use
Complexity




AsPredicted
Simple hypothesis-testing studies
Low


OSF Registries
General-purpose preregistration for any study type
Medium


PROSPERO
Systematic reviews and meta-analyses
Medium


Registered Reports Center (COS)
Registered Reports and participating journals
High


Open Science Framework Registered Report Templates
Detailed preregistrations and Stage-1 RR submissions
High


ClinicalTrials.gov
Clinical trials and intervention studies
High







9.3.1 Minimal preregistration
In a minimal preregistration, the key claims tested will be identified. This should include

Research question(s)
Hypotheses
Primary outcome variable(s)
Planned sample size
Main statistical test(s)

Several online repositories, such as AsPredicted and OSF Registries, support study preregistration (see Table 9.1). These services provide structured templates that guide researchers in documenting the key elements of their study design, hypotheses, and analysis plans. Once submitted, the preregistration is stored as a time-stamped record, creating verifiable evidence that the research plan existed before data collection or analysis.






NoteMinimal preregistration example



A good example of preregistration in music psychology is a study about music emotion recognition and specific traits in early childhood by Paz et al. (2025). They wanted to examine children aged 3 to 5 and whether those children who score high on callous-unemotional trait have poorer recognition ability than others. Their preregistration at aspredicted.org covers the design, sample size, and the main analysis (including some exploratory analyses) strategy, although it is by no means comprehensive. The results showed that indeed children higher on callous‐unemotional traits showed poorer emotion recognition scores.


Most preregistration services also provide options for embargoing or anonymising registrations, allowing researchers to preserve the requirements of double-blind peer review while still benefiting from the transparency afforded by preregistration.


9.3.2 Full preregistration
In a full preregistration, researchers attempt to document all major decisions that relate to the study before collecting data. This will usually include:

Detailed study design and procedure
Recruitment criteria
Inclusion and exclusion criteria
Sample size justification (e.g., power analysis)
Music (or other stimuli) used
Variables collected
Data preprocessing steps
Handling of missing data
Outlier criteria
Statistical models
Covariates (if any)
Multiple-comparison corrections
Planned exploratory analyses







NoteFull preregistration example



Lowe-Brown et al. (2026) tested whether playlists that gradually transition from music matching a listener’s current mood to music reflecting a desired mood are more effective for emotion regulation than alternative playlist structures. The authors preregistered their hypotheses, design, outcome measures, and analysis plan in OSF before collecting data, thereby clearly distinguishing confirmatory tests of the so-called iso principle from subsequent exploratory analyses. The study found only limited support for the iso principle, demonstrating how preregistration can increase the credibility of findings by ensuring that null or mixed results are reported transparently rather than being obscured through post hoc hypothesis modification.




9.3.3 Registered Reports
Registered Reports take preregistration one step further than the approaches described above. In this format, a complete study proposal—including the background, aims, design, materials, and analysis plan—is submitted to a journal that accepts Registered Reports. At this stage, no data have been collected or analysed.
This submission, known as the Stage 1 manuscript, undergoes peer review. Reviewers evaluate the importance of the research question, the quality of the study design, the adequacy of the planned analyses, and the overall rigour of the proposed methodology. This adds an important layer of quality control to preregistration, as weaknesses in the design, sampling strategy, materials, or analysis plan can be identified and addressed before data collection begins.
Following Stage 1 review, the journal may reject the manuscript, request revisions, or grant in-principle acceptance (IPA). An IPA means that the journal commits to publishing the study regardless of the outcome, provided that the approved protocol is followed, any deviations are transparently documented, and the conclusions are supported by the evidence.
After receiving IPA, researchers collect and analyse the data according to the approved protocol. Any departures from the original plan should be clearly justified and documented. The completed manuscript, now including the results and discussion, is then submitted for Stage 2 review. At this stage, reviewers assess whether the study was conducted as approved, whether deviations have been adequately reported, and whether the conclusions are justified by the findings. Importantly, publication does not depend on whether the results are statistically significant, novel, or supportive of the original hypotheses.



Schematic outline of the Registered Reports process


Beyond reducing systematic bias and publication bias, Registered Reports offer an additional benefit: expert feedback is obtained before data collection begins. This allows reviewers to improve aspects of the study that are difficult or impossible to change later, such as the design, stimuli, sampling strategy, statistical analyses, or power considerations.






NoteRegistered Report example



Lahdelma and Eerola (2024) used the Registered Reports format to investigate the emotional connotations of musical chords (major, minor, augmented, diminished, and suspended fourth chords) using an affective priming paradigm. Their 9-page Stage 1 manuscript contained a detailed description of the study rationale, hypotheses, methods, stimuli, procedure, sample size justification, planned analyses, inclusion and exclusion criteria, and treatment of outliers.
During Stage 1 review, peer reviewers suggested several improvements to the hypotheses, stimulus selection criteria, and planned analyses. Following in-principle acceptance, the researchers collected data from the planned sample of 400 participants and conducted the analyses according to the approved protocol.
Not all preregistered hypotheses were supported. For example, the data did not support the prediction that suspended fourth chords would produce the expected affective priming effects. This illustrates one of the central strengths of the Registered Reports format: studies are published regardless of whether the results confirm the original hypotheses, provided that the approved protocol has been followed and the conclusions are supported by the evidence. In a public blog post, the authors reflected positively on the Registered Reports process, highlighting the value of receiving methodological feedback before data collection, while also noting the additional time and effort required to prepare and revise the Stage 1 manuscript.


It is worth noting that, currently, only one journal in the field of music supports Registered Reports: (Music Perception). An alternative route to publishing a Registered Report is offered by Peer Community In Registered Reports (PCI RR). PCI RR is an independent peer-review platform that evaluates Registered Reports in a manner similar to the Stage 1 review process used by journals. If the proposal successfully passes review, PCI RR issues an In-Principle Recommendation (IPR), which functions similarly to the in-principle acceptance granted by a journal. Researchers then conduct the study according to the approved protocol and submit the completed manuscript for Stage 2 review. A growing number of PCI RR-friendly journals have agreed to consider manuscripts that have successfully completed the PCI RR review process.

title: 9  Preregistration
text:
9.4 Summary
For music research, a minimal preregistration is already a substantial improvement over no preregistration. Full preregistration becomes particularly valuable when a study involves numerous or complex analytical decisions, such as feature selection in music information retrieval studies, psychophysiological analyses, longitudinal designs, or scale-development projects. Metascientific research has shown that preregistration has increased the quality of scholarship in the areas where it has been used (Ofosu & Posner, 2023; Van Den Akker et al., 2024).
However, preregistration should not be viewed as a solution for every type of study. It is most valuable when evaluating predefined hypotheses and claims. Research that is primarily exploratory, interpretative, or practice-based may not be well suited to comprehensive preregistration. In other words, preregistration is less useful when the primary goal is exploration, discovery, interpretation, or creative practice rather than hypothesis testing.
In such cases, transparency remains equally important and can be promoted through other open research practices, including sharing materials and data, maintaining open notebooks, documenting analytical decisions, and providing reflexive accounts of the research process. These approaches help make the development of ideas, interpretations, and conclusions more transparent, even when preregistration is neither feasible nor appropriate.

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.2 7.2 What is replication?
The term replication is used in a variety of ways in scientific discourse, and this variety can create confusion when researchers, reviewers, and readers evaluate claims about whether a finding has or has not replicated. Nosek and Errington (2020) propose a clarifying definition: a replication is an investigation that minimally alters the original circumstances while maintaining the same theoretical prediction. The goal of a replication is therefore not simply to reproduce the same numerical outcomes, but to test whether the same theoretical inference can be drawn from new evidence. Whether a replication succeeds or fails depends not on whether the statistics match, but on whether the conditions gave the original claim a genuine opportunity to be confirmed or disconfirmed.
This framing also clarifies an important distinction between two related but conceptually separate concepts: reproducibility and replicability (Nosek & Errington, 2020). Reproducibility refers to the ability to obtain the same analytical results from the same data using the same code and procedures. A failure of reproducibility—for example, when a reported analysis cannot be reproduced from the shared dataset—indicates a problem with the analysis or reporting rather than with the underlying empirical claim. Replicability, by contrast, refers to whether a new study, conducted with a fresh sample and potentially varying some procedural details, arrives at a similar conclusion. Failures of reproducibility and failures of replicability are distinct problems that call for different solutions.
In music research, both forms of transparency matter. Reproducible research requires sharing data, analysis code, and sufficient procedural detail for others to verify the reported results (see ?sec-OAdata). Replicable research requires that original findings be robust enough to emerge in new samples and contexts, and that procedures are reported with enough specificity for others to implement them. A study that uses commercially licensed music recordings, withholds participant-level data, or describes stimuli only vaguely poses challenges for both reproducibility and replication—challenges that open materials practices are designed to address.

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.3 7.3 Types of replication
Replication studies are not a uniform category. The degree to which a replication attempt preserves or varies the methods of the original study has important consequences for what can be inferred from its results. Several typologies have been proposed; among the most commonly used is the distinction between direct and conceptual replication, extended by LeBel et al. (lebel2018unified?; lebel2019brief?) into a more detailed taxonomy that grades replication studies by their procedural, sample, and contextual similarity to the original.

7.3.1 7.3.1 Direct replication
In a direct replication, the researcher aims to reproduce the original study as closely as possible, using the same or highly similar procedures, stimuli, measures, and participant population. The goal is to determine whether the original findings can be observed again under closely matched conditions. A direct replication that fails to reproduce the original result provides evidence that the effect may have been a false positive, that the original effect size was overestimated, or that some uncontrolled feature of the original study contributed to the outcome.
In music research, direct replication is complicated by the fact that stimuli are often inadequately reported or unavailable. When stimuli consist of commercially recorded music, sharing the original audio files may be legally constrained, and descriptions of excerpt boundaries, recording versions, or presentation parameters are frequently omitted from published reports. This underlines the importance of open materials practices: publishing stimuli directly, or reporting them in sufficient detail for others to approximate the original conditions, is a prerequisite for enabling meaningful direct replication.






Direct replication example
The infamous Mozart effect—the claim that brief exposure to Mozart’s music temporarily enhances spatial reasoning ability—was originally reported by Rauscher, Shaw, and Ky (rauscher1993music?) and attracted substantial media attention and policy interest. Subsequent attempts to replicate the effect produced inconsistent results. Chabris (chabris1999prelude?) argued on the basis of available evidence that no reliable Mozart effect could be demonstrated, and a meta-analysis by Pietschnig, Voracek, and Formann (pietschnig2010mozart?) examining data from over 39 studies concluded that while a small effect could be detected in aggregate, it was substantially smaller and more fragile than originally claimed, and likely reflected arousal or mood rather than a specific musical enhancement of spatial cognition. The Mozart effect thus illustrates both how a single unreplicated finding can achieve disproportionate public influence, and how systematic replication efforts are necessary to establish the robustness of empirical claims in music psychology.





7.3.2 7.3.2 Conceptual replication
A conceptual replication tests the same theoretical claim using different methods, stimuli, measures, or populations. Rather than reproducing the original study, the aim is to determine whether the underlying prediction holds when operationalised in a different way. Conceptual replications provide evidence for the generalisability of a finding but are more difficult to interpret when they fail: a conceptual failure to replicate could indicate that the original theory is wrong, or simply that the new operationalisation does not adequately capture the same construct.
In music psychology, conceptual replication frequently takes the form of cross-cultural studies that examine whether findings obtained with Western participants and Western musical materials generalise to listeners from different musical traditions. Cross-cultural studies of music and emotion perception, for example, have tested whether the emotional qualities attributed to musical stimuli are consistent across listeners with varying degrees of exposure to Western tonal music. Such studies do not reproduce the original conditions, but they test whether the same theoretical claim—that certain musical features reliably signal certain emotional qualities—holds across different contexts.
Adversarial collaboration, in which researchers holding opposing views design a study that both parties consider a fair test, is a related strategy that can strengthen the credibility of conceptual replications. By agreeing in advance on what evidence would support or refute each position, adversarial collaborators reduce the risk that a failed conceptual replication will be dismissed on methodological grounds [e.g., axt2026relationship; binnendyk2025overconfidence; matzke2015horizontal].






Conceptual replication example
The emotional associations of musical mode—the widely held claim that major keys sound happy and minor keys sound sad—have been subject to extensive conceptual replication across cultural contexts. While this association is robust among listeners with Western musical enculturation, studies examining listeners from non-Western musical traditions have produced more mixed results. Egermann et al. (egermann2015universal?) compared emotional responses to music in Western and Congolese participants and found that mode-based emotional attributions were substantially weaker in the Congolese sample, suggesting that the association may depend on enculturation rather than universal auditory features. This kind of cross-cultural work serves as a conceptual replication by testing whether the same theoretical claim holds in a population for which the original conditions—Western listeners, Western tonal music—do not straightforwardly apply.

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.4 7.4 Single studies and multi-lab replications
Replication studies vary not only in the degree to which they reproduce original methods but also in scale. A single-lab replication—conducted by one team in one location—can provide useful evidence about whether a finding holds in a new sample, but its conclusions are themselves limited by sample size and local context. A successful single-lab replication may still be a false positive; a failed one may reflect local conditions rather than a genuine failure of the original effect. Multi-lab replications, in which many independent research teams test the same hypothesis using a coordinated protocol, can provide substantially more reliable evidence, and additionally allow researchers to examine whether effect sizes vary across sites, samples, and cultural settings.
Large-scale collaborative replication initiatives have become increasingly common in psychology. The ManyLabs projects (klein2014investigating?; klein2018many?) coordinated replication attempts across dozens of sites and multiple countries, enabling systematic examination of replicability and cross-site variability. The Psychological Science Accelerator [PSA; (moshontz2018psychological?)] provides ongoing institutional infrastructure for large-scale, globally distributed psychological research, including studies that would be impractical for any single laboratory to conduct.
Music psychology has begun to engage with multi-lab and large-scale approaches, though coordinated multi-site replications remain relatively rare in the field. Cross-cultural studies offer one practical model: by collecting data at multiple geographically and culturally distinct sites, researchers can simultaneously test whether effects replicate across samples and assess the moderating role of cultural background. Large-scale online data collection has also created new opportunities to reach larger and more diverse samples than are typically accessible in single-laboratory research, making it more feasible to detect small or variable effects and to test the boundary conditions of theoretical claims.






Multi-lab replication example
Mehr et al. (mehr2019universality?) coordinated a large cross-cultural study testing claims about universals in human song, collecting behavioural data from participants across sixty societies, including communities with limited prior exposure to Western music. The study tested theoretical predictions about the formal properties of music that might be universal—such as the use of discrete pitches, small pitch intervals, and regular rhythmic organisation—and about the extent to which listeners unfamiliar with a song’s cultural origin can nonetheless infer its social function (e.g., lullaby vs. dance song). By coordinating data collection across sites with diverse populations and using a shared stimulus set, the project provided a substantially stronger and more generalisable test of universality claims than would have been possible within any single laboratory, while also documenting the considerable cultural variation in musical features that co-exists with apparent universals.

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.5 7.5 Qualitative research and replication
The discussion of replication in music research extends beyond quantitative experimental designs to include qualitative and interpretive approaches. Qualitative methods—including interviews, ethnographies, case studies, and thematic analysis—are well established in musicology, ethnomusicology, music education, and increasingly in music psychology. What replication means in these contexts requires careful consideration, since qualitative research is not typically designed to test hypotheses about population-level effects in the way that experimental studies are.
Tuval-Mashiach (2021) argues that replication in qualitative research is both meaningful and valuable, but requires a different conceptual framework. Because qualitative research is generally concerned with understanding the particular—the lived experience of specific participants, the meanings embedded in specific musical contexts—replication is not aimed at reproducing the same findings in a new sample. Instead, Tuval-Mashiach distinguishes between methodological replication, which applies the same or similar analytical procedures to a new sample to test whether comparable themes or patterns emerge, and theoretical replication, which tests whether theoretical propositions that developed in one context hold in a different context with different participants or settings. A third mode, reflexive replication, involves revisiting a previously analysed dataset with different analytic assumptions or frameworks, examining whether alternative interpretations are defensible.
These principles apply directly to qualitative and mixed-methods work in music research. Phenomenological studies of musical experience, interview-based investigations of music listening, or thematic analyses of the functions of music in everyday life may be difficult to replicate in any strict procedural sense, but their credibility is enhanced when researchers document analytical procedures transparently, provide access to data where ethically permissible, and situate their interpretations in relation to existing theoretical frameworks. Transparency about the research process—including decisions about sampling, coding scheme development, and interpretation—serves a function analogous to that of preregistration in quantitative research (see ?sec-registration): it allows readers to evaluate how conclusions were reached and to assess whether the reported themes are likely to reflect the data rather than researcher expectations.






Qualitative replication example
DeNora (denora2000music?) used ethnographic interviews to document how individuals use music as a resource for emotion regulation, identity construction, and the management of everyday environments. The theoretical account that emerged from this work—of music as an affordance for self-regulation—has subsequently been examined using comparable qualitative approaches across diverse populations and cultural settings, including studies of music use among adolescents, older adults, and clinical populations. Although these follow-on studies were not designed as formal replications and varied considerably in their methods, together they constitute a form of theoretical replication: the same core propositions about music’s self-regulatory functions recur across studies using different samples, interview protocols, and analytic approaches. The consistency of these themes across contexts strengthens confidence in the underlying theoretical framework, even in the absence of the confirmatory statistical logic that governs quantitative replication.

title: 7  7 Replication Studies
text:
7.6 7.6 Summary
Replication is fundamental to the credibility of scientific findings. For music research, engaging seriously with replication means attending both to whether existing findings can be reproduced from shared data and code, and to whether theoretical claims hold when tested in new samples and contexts. Both forms of scrutiny require a commitment to transparency in reporting methods, stimuli, and analytical decisions.
The diversity of replication types—direct, conceptual, single-lab, multi-lab—reflects the fact that replication is not a single practice but a family of related strategies for testing the robustness of empirical claims. Direct replications evaluate whether findings persist under closely matched conditions; conceptual replications assess whether theoretical claims generalise across different operationalisations and populations; multi-lab replications provide evidence about both average robustness and cross-site variability. Each approach has different strengths and limitations, and a mature research programme typically benefits from contributions of several kinds.
For music research, replication poses distinctive practical challenges. Stimuli are frequently underspecified or unavailable; participant samples are often small and culturally restricted; and musical experience depends on factors—cultural background, personal history, familiarity—that are difficult to control or match across studies. These challenges do not make replication impossible, but they do underline the importance of open materials practices and thoughtful stimulus reporting as preconditions for meaningful replication attempts.
Replication is not equally appropriate for all types of music research. For exploratory, interpretive, or practice-based work, formal replication may be neither feasible nor the right standard of evidence. In these cases, transparency about the research process—documenting analytical decisions, sharing data and materials where possible, providing reflexive accounts of interpretation—serves analogous purposes. Replication, like preregistration, is most valuable when the goal is to test predefined theoretical claims. It should be understood as one among several complementary open science practices that together strengthen the credibility, generalisability, and accessibility of music research.




Nosek, B. A., & Errington, T. M. (2020). What is replication? PLoS Biology, 18(3), e3000691. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000691


Tuval-Mashiach, R. (2021). Is replication relevant for qualitative research? Qualitative Psychology, 8(3), 365. https://doi.org/10.1037/qup0000217
